content: What are the outstanding items? I see that some parts do not look finished yet.
I'll republish after finishing all part.
All right! Thank you. 🙂
I revised the statement a little and published it. If there are any errors or things that need to be corrected, please let me know.

I'll take a closer look later today, but at first glance it seems the stub is missing. You'll need to place "stub.txt" in the config folder.

I'd also like to review the contents of "config.ini" to confirm that the configuration is set up correctly.

Additionally, you might consider adding an opening sequence in the viewer, as that's typically included in most viewer-enabled puzzles.

Thanks.
Also, just curious: is this puzzle your original idea?

This game was actually inspired by Olymbits’ mechanism where all games move in the same direction at the same time, so I cannot really say it is entirely my original idea. That is why I hesitated a lot at first about whether to publish it. However, after increasing the N limit a little, it seemed like an interesting heuristic problem, so I decided to share it. If there is anything problematic, I will take the post down.
No worries! It's completely fine, and it's a brilliant idea! You might even consider adding the background story as a fun fact in the statement.
I'll consider about it. Thanks for the interesting idea!

+ Updated stub.txt, it is now displaying correctly.
+ Slightly changed the cell colors for visibility: invalid empty cells are bright red, initially filled cells are gray, and all other cells are white.

All sections
* Please change "sudoku" to "Sudoku".

Goal
* Please change "that share the same" to ", each with a".

* Please move the sections "How your board is applied" and "Scoring" before the Input section, to be consistent with the typical section order used on CodinGame.

Scoring
* Please replace "that line" with "that row / column / block", since a block is not a line.

* Please add the clarification "(4 + 5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9)" after 36.

* Please remove "the line" before both example strings "123123123" and "123456789".

* Consider randomising the digit order in the example "123456789" to imply that digit ordering is irrelevant.

Constraints
* Please remove the second constraint (9 characters long) as it is already stated in the Input section.

* Could you clarify how the 1-second time limit was chosen? Was it designed with both fast and slow programming languages in mind? (This is just a question for understanding the choice. I don't have any preferences yet.)

Cases
* These cases are the same:
  • test 5
  • validator 14
  Please modify them so that they are different.

* Consider adding a new test (not validator) or modifying an existing test to include more than 1 empty Sudoku grid, since this scenario currently appears in validators only.

* Across many cases, some Sudoku grids are reused and even follow the same order of appearance. While the cases still differ due to different values of [[N]] or appear with a different combination of puzzles (except for the duplicate pair mentioned above), this may unintentionally favour solutions with certain search strategies.

Consider increasing variety by introducing new grids, as well as rotated and/or flipped versions of existing grids.

* There are currently 20 Sudoku grids:
  - 7 have a unique solution.
  - 3 have a small number of solutions (4, 10 and 19).
  - 2 have dozens of solutions (69 and 71).
  - 8 have 100+ solutions (including one empty grid).
  
  Grids with many solutions impose very weak constraints, since they allow many valid completions. These 100+ solution grids appear very frequently (in 9 out of 15 validators). They reduce the overall difficulty and result in a smoother optimisation landscape.

  A more balanced distribution would improve difficulty, e.g.
  - some tests with uniform mix
  - some biased toward low-solution grids
  - some mixed-pressure configurations

* Consider adding grids with a medium number of solutions (around 30 to 50) to improve balance between rigidity and flexibility.

Referee / Viewer
* As mentioned earlier, consider adding an opening sequence in the viewer that runs automatically when the page is loaded or refreshed. Example: https://www.codingame.com/contribute/view/1387470f2e91c15e164db756ef163819c5862d .

* Consider increasing the size of the Sudoku grid and improving font sizes for readability. In particular, row/column missing-digit counts are currently difficult to read.

* I'll perform some additional testing later this week.

Thanks.

I will respond to your points.

I agree that a 1-second time limit is punishing for slow languages such as Python. However, increasing the time limit would also increase the amount of computation for faster languages like C++ and Rust, and if we raise the number of test cases to 30, a single submission could take up to 90 seconds. That is a concern for me.

I also have a couple of questions.
1. You mentioned that there can be multiple valid solutions. Is this problem easy to solve optimally? If it is too easy, I am worried that too many perfect scores might be possible.
2. By “opening sequence,” do you mean the animation shown on the main page or in the CodinGame IDE? If so, would it be okay to visualize and show a few predefined sample boards?

Time limit
* I agree that the trade-off exists and is common in many bot and optimisation puzzles on CodinGame. It'd be useful to outstand how far solutions can realistically be optimised within a 1-second time limit. Have you tried implementing your own solver to gauge what level of performance is achievable?

Perfect scores
* I'm not entirely sure, but near-optimal clustering seems quite likely. To reduce this, you could (you've done some of these already):

  - increase conflict between grids (so no single choice works well everywhere),
  - adjust the distribution toward more medium-constraint grids,
  - and use duplicates strategically to create competing "preferences" across puzzles.

Open sequence
* I meant in the IDE. Showing a few predefined sample boards would work well and could help users quickly understand how their output is applied and evaluated.

Perfect scores
* Increasing the number and variety of test cases also makes it harder for a single heuristic to perform uniformly well, which helps reduce the risk of solutions converging to very similar (near-optimal) scores. For example, a test case set is good if a simple strategy improves the scores on some cases while it worsens results on others.
Thank you for the feedback. I will make the project private for now, and once I have finished applying the changes you mentioned, I will publish it again. I guess it will take about 1-2 days.
No problem!

+changed the font family to prevent the text from getting cut off.
+When entering the IDE for the first time, I made it cycle through three demo boards. I used one correct board and two incorrect boards as examples.
+increased the number of validators to 35. I added randomization and also added/removed existing boards, but I am still not sure whether they are good boards. Please take a look.
+increased the time limit to 3s.
+I have finished revising the problem statement as requested.
By the way, I felt making optimization problem is really difficult. Thank you so much for your big help with the major revisions.
I fixed a bug where the 3s time limit was not being applied. Most of the major visible issues seem to have been fixed as well.

That's great progress and a strong improvement!

I'm genuinely impressed. Given that you haven't created a simpler, text-only contribution before, jumping straight into a more complex, viewer-enabled puzzle is a big step. There are many more moving parts to manage, but you've handled a lot of them well on your first attempt. You've done a really solid job here, so you should be proud of yourself!

The next message contains my comments on the updated contribution. The most critical area that needs change at this moment is the cases.

Scoring
* Yikes, what was I thinking yesterday? I'm sorry about my wrong suggestion; please change 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9 to 6².

Constraints
* I don't have a firm view on the ideal time limit yet. It'd be helpful to wait for feedback from other reviewers before finalising this.

In addition, have you tried writing and timing your solution? If so, could you please share the results and the programming language used? Does your code use the full time limit before output?

Configuration
* Please share the contents of "config.ini" here. I'd like to check that the configuration is set up correctly.

Cases
* There are currently 34 Sudoku grids:
  - 13 have a unique solution.
  - 3 have a small number of solutions (4, 10 and 19).
  - 2 have dozens of solutions (69 and 71).
  - 16 have 100+ solutions (including one empty grid).
  
  Increasing the total number of grids from 20 to 34 is a good step. However, the issue remains that there aren't any grids with a medium number of solutions (say 30 to 50 solutions).

  If you don't already have one, you'll likely need a single-grid Sudoku solver to estimate the number of solutions per grid.

  Specific revision suggestions are as follows.

* Overall goal: fewer "all-flexible" cases, and more cases where medium-constraint grids and duplicates create conflicting preferences.

* Replace a varying number (1 to 5) of the 100+ solution grids with medium-solution grids. Some medium-solution grids can appear more than once.
  - Test 5
  - Validators 12, 15, 16, 21, 25, 27, 30, 32, 33, 34, 35

* Modify validators to include duplicate grids.

  - Introduce duplicates progressively from validator 15 onward, increasing both:
    • the number of duplicated grids
    • and how many times they appear in a single case

  - Aim to include different types of duplication:
    • duplicates of unique-solution grids
    • duplicates of medium-solution grids
    • duplicates of 100+-solution grids

* Add Test 6 based on Test 3, but with weighted duplication:
  - Grid 1 appears once
  - Grid 2 appears twice
  - Grid 3 appears 3 times
  - Grid 4 appears 4 times
  - Grid 5 appears 5 times
  - Shuffle the order of grids

* Add a validator:
  - A unique-solution grid appears once
  - A medium-solution grid appears twice
  - A 100+-solution non-empty grid appears 3 times
  - Another unique-solution grid appears 4 times
  - Another medium-solution grid appears 5 times
  - Shuffle the order of grids

* As a general note, problems often have an equal number of tests and validators. That's not strictly necessary for this puzzle, especially if the visible tests are representative. However, some reviewers may have a different opinion. It can also help players better gauge their performance if the visible tests resemble the validators. I'll leave it to you whether to adjust this. Alternatively, we can wait for more feedback before taking any action.

Referee / Viewer
* I'll perform some additional testing later this week.

Thanks.

By the way, no need to keep toggling the contribution between WIP and Draft/Private 😆 I can still access it while it's in Draft. If you'd prefer to keep it private until it reaches a point you're happy with, feel free to leave it in that state.

Ok. I will keep this project private until you think it's perfect.

And here's some answer for your questions:
* Config.ini
I hope this format is right.

title=Multiple Sudokus
min_players=1
max_players=1
type=opti
criteria=score
criteria_en=Total cost
criteria_fr=Coût total
sorting_order=asc

* My solution
Of course, I have Python and C++ code, but it have only simply operations yet. here are some comparison. both code have same logic and hash number.

C++/Python
Score : 23.4k vs 62.2k
Iteration(test 5) : 2305800/50500

I am not very good at Python, so the numbers are a bit extreme. :/

Thanks, this looks good overall.

Config.ini
* The most important settings all look correct. The criteria_en and criteria_fr fields are just display labels, so they're less important for functionality. You could even omit both of them if you prefer, especially since "Total cost" isn't strictly a precise description of the scoring.

French version
* By the way, have you included a French version of the statement? If so, note that it's optional; you can safely omit it if maintaining it becomes too much overhead.

Statistics of your solution
* No worries! Just to confirm, were the reported scores and iteration counts obtained using the full 3-second time limit in both C++ and Python?

Thanks.

Oops, I forgot to send the zip file, and I was only testing it in the IDE. I uploaded it right away.

-French version
I do not know French, so I translated it with an LLM. It might be better to remove it.
-Config.ini
I will delete both of the ones you mentioned.
-Statistics
Using the full 3 seconds made my solutions time out, so I tested with a 2.9-second limit.

+Updated!
May I ask how you approximated the number of Sudoku solutions? I am having trouble constructing the cases. :(

Thanks, that all sounds reasonable.

Using a 2.9s limit is fine for now. it's close enough to the actual limit and still gives a good sense of performance, especially since we're mainly trying to gauge relative behaviour rather than exact tuning at this stage.

Also, do you have a rough idea of how close your best score is to the theoretical optimum? I assume it won't reach 0 in general due to unavoidable conflicts between grids, but it would be useful to understand how large the remaining gap is in practice. If your first attempt already gets very close to the optimum in C++, that might suggest the 3s limit is a bit generous.

I use my Sudoku solver to count the number of solutions. It's based on the code here: https://www.cs.mcgill.ca/~aassaf9/python/algorithm_x.html

I found this just now if you need an online tool to help you create new Sudoku puzzles with a specific number of solutions without having to write a solver yourself: https://sigh.github.io/Interactive-Sudoku-Solver

The following are the results of testing my C++ solution with 10 different seeds on samples 1–6.

Variance
test 1 - min: 0 / max: 0 / std: 0
test 2 - min: 30 / max: 35 / std: ±1.5
test 3 - min: 212 / max: 227 / std: ±5.3
test 4 - min: 441 / max: 492 / std: ±15.1
test 5 - min: 1263 / max: 1376 / std: ±36.2
test 6 - min: 607 / max: 680 / std: ±22

Best score by time limit
test 1 - 0.5s: 0 / 1.0s: 0 / 1.5s: 0 / 2.0s: 0 / 2.9s: 0
test 2 - 0.5s: 30 / 1.0s: 30 / 1.5s: 30 / 2.0s: 30 / 2.9s: 30
test 3 - 0.5s: 271 / 1.0s: 215 / 1.5s: 213 / 2.0s: 213 / 2.9s: 213
test 4 - 0.5s: 1054 / 1.0s: 519 / 1.5s: 462 / 2.0s: 462 / 2.9s: 462
test 5 - 0.5s: 2168 / 1.0s: 2182 / 1.5s: 1644 / 2.0s: 1303 / 2.9s: 1303
test 6 - 0.5s: 1356 / 1.0s: 687 / 1.5s: 641 / 2.0s: 641 / 2.9s: 641

I have identified two possible explanations here:

1. I may not be very good at simulated annealing, or
2. the problem itself may be difficult to optimize.

I would like to hear your opinion on which of these you think is more likely.

Thanks, this is very useful data.

From what I see, the behaviour looks more consistent with the problem being genuinely hard to optimise rather than just an issue with simulated annealing quality. The fact that improvements often plateau early (especially in tests 3-6) and that additional time does not consistently improve results suggests the search space may have many local optima or fairly flat regions where heuristics stop gaining traction.

At the same time, the marginal gains beyond 2.0-2.9s look quite small in several tests, which suggests that a shorter time limit (e.g. around 1s) might still be sufficient in practice. It may be worth temporarily lowering it while gathering more feedback from other reviewers, just to better calibrate the intended difficulty.

No need to upload any revised zip files immediately; you may wait until you've adjusted the cases.
The cases currently uploaded are the revised ones. Should I change this problem to WIP so I can gather more opinions from other people?
It's up to you. If you feel ready to receive more feedback and update the contribution without switching it to Draft/Private first, it's absolutely fine to change the problem to WIP.
Okay. I'll change the status to WIP. Thanks for your help over the past few days.
No problem! I'll take another look later on, and will do some testing as well.

General
* I tried outputting more than 9 digits in a line. The referee didn't flag this as invalid; it simply ignored the extra digits. If this is the intended behaviour, you may want to mention it in the Output section. Otherwise, the referee should be updated to reject such outputs.

(Note that extra output *lines* is another matter; they're always ignored by default and you can't do anything about that.)

Constraints
* As noted earlier, the 3-second time limit appears somewhat generous based on your C++ results. It may be worth temporarily reducing it (e.g. back to 1 second) while gathering further feedback from other reviewers.

Viewer
* In the header, please change "cost" to "score".

Thanks.

After the patch, the score has slightly increased from 24.3k to 25.4k.
Additionally, it is no longer allowed to output more than 9 numbers in a single line. However, for cells that are already fully determined and do not require an explicit number, either '.' or any digit may be printed.
The term "cost" has been replaced with "score".
